videos.title: Our vibe coded projects that actually work | The Vergecast
videos.description: It’s time for a new series on The Vergecast! (It still needs a name. Please help.) We’re going to give Verge staffers a challenge, and regroup a few weeks later to see who did it best. We’re starting with some vibe coding. The Verge’s Jake Kastrenakes and Hayden Field share what they’ve made with AI that has actually stuck in their lives, before David gives the challenge: build a website to solve a problem in your life. The more ambitious and impressive the better. We’ll be back with the results

videos.title: RAMageddon has come for Apple | The Vergecast
videos.description: Meta's business is doing just fine. But Meta as a company, and Meta as a series of products? That is, uh, messier. David and Nilay discuss the company's ongoing desire to be relevant and cool, the unceasing importance of Instagram, and why it makes perfect sense that Facebook would clone Polymarket. After that, the hosts talk about Apple's huge price increases, and the ways in which RAMageddon might change the gadget market forever. videos.title: 2026 Dell XPS: The laptop that can balance it all | Hype Desk
videos.description:
[Advertiser content from Dell]

For years, the dream of a do-everything workstation required a trade-off between raw horsepower and the battery life needed for true portability. The 2026 Dell XPS laptop changes that conversation. From its refined CNC machined aluminum chassis to its 2.8K tandem OLED display—not to mention smart thermals and a long battery life—every detail is engineered for those who need productivity on the go. videos.title: YouTube as an AI training force #Vergecast
videos.description:
AI models are learning from YouTube at scale because downloading videos is easy. So why hasn’t YouTube stopped it? Atlantic reporter Alex Reisner joins us on The Vergecast today to explain.

videos.title: How to train your data | The Vergecast
videos.description: Training data is the raw material of the AI industry. Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, and the rest are built on top of oceans of stuff. What is that stuff? Books. Blog posts. YouTube videos. Reddit comments. All of it and more, in virtually incomprehensible quantities. Alex Reisner, a staff writer at The Atlantic who has been investigating training data, explains how AI companies get all this data, why they'd really prefer you not know what's in it, and whether training data could ever be a fair trade.
